So I was riding my bike home from college one day and because I didn't have enough money for another car, it's been open, okay? And as I was riding my bike home, I was beating myself up. I was like, what is wrong with me? I know what I would do in that guy's scenario. I know what I would do in that lady's scenario. And I was riding my bike home and it was the first time in my life when I ever realized there is a difference between sales and marketing. What? And that sounds cheesy as I say that now and some of you guys are probably like, well, duh, but I had never considered that. I thought it was the same thing. I thought marketing was my logos. I thought marketing was just kind of, you know, getting out there, getting attention. That's not marketing at all, okay? Sales, marketing and closing. Those are three very separate activities that you need to become good at, okay? Marketing, sales, closing. So what I wanted to do real quick is define what those things are so that you know what they're not. Okay, because marketing is not logos. Marketing is not how the page looks. Marketing is not freaking business cards. I will never have a business card unless there's some kind of call to action on it. Like they're so dumb, in my opinion. Okay, I'm just gonna say it. I can't measure progress from it. I come from the direct response world. I can't measure response. Okay, that's why business, what? And I know some of you guys come from the corporate world or some of you guys are like, what? This guy's crazy. Anyway, I just want you to understand, like from a marketers perspective, if I can't measure the return on money coming back in, why am I gonna go spend money on it? Okay, all right. So that's like more advertising, but sometimes we'll confuse advertising with marketing. Again, not the same thing. They're not the same thing. They're all kind of intertwined here and little places there, but they're very, very, very separate activities. Number one, what is marketing? Marketing, this is my own definition, but I've never been able to disprove it. Marketing is the act of changing beliefs with the intent of a sale. That's it. That's all marketing is. It's the act of changing beliefs with the intent of a sale. Now, what have I been doing to you this entire first week? Changing your beliefs. How am I doing it? I know exactly how I'm doing it. That's what I'm gonna share with you guys today. Sales is presenting an offer and overcoming objections. That's when you're in sales zone. Here's my offer and people are like, oh, but I have this excuse not to buy. And I have this excuse not to buy. And they're like, I see your excuse not to buy. Here's my reason why you still should. And I'm gonna go in for the close. I see your excuse, right? And that's your overcoming objections. Okay, so marketing, the act of changing beliefs with the intent of a sale. Sales is presenting the offer and overcoming objections. All closing is, which do not skip closing. Cash is in the closing. Okay, so we actually get paid. Okay? Closing is logical reasons why you should act now. That's all closing is. Buy one, get one free. Right? They're half off only till noon today. Okay? Does that make sense? Refer a friend and get this amount as well. Mail and rebate offer. Okay? Those are all logical reasons why you should act now. But that's not the same thing as selling. And it's not the same thing as marketing. And when I started learning the difference between those two, my wallet got notably fatter. Okay? I just want to, this is a big deal to understand this. Okay? What makes someone good on the internet is marketing. Okay? What makes someone, what makes someone to sell really, really well, right? Is if they've been marketed to first. And there's a gap opened in my brain. And I have to fill the gap. And things are not as I thought they were. The gap is now opened. And then number three, closing. Closing is just logical reasons to act now. That's all it is. Okay? You're like, I'm not a closer. When those hormones after they buy start to die down, their left brain kicks back in. Crap! Why did I buy that? Anyone ever done that as you walk out of a store? I should have bought that thing. What happened? Emotions and hormones overrode the left side of the brain and as you walked out, they burn off and the logical is still there. And it's like, oh, wait, wait, wait, why did I do this? Oh crap. And then they have to start justifying why they did what they did. If they justify what they did, then it was a successful purchase. If they don't, that's where buyers remorse enters in. So there are three, if you guys have a piece of paper, which if you don't, I don't know why. Get a piece of paper, take notes on this. The brain experiences sales and the sales process typically in this order. And the conversation looks like this. Step one, you know what? I see that there's a possibility that that product could work. It's kind of like a general agreement that that product could actually do what it says it's supposed to. Steve Jobs, I believe you that this phone, it looks like it works. How many of you guys believe the iPhone worked and would be amazing before you bought it? Everybody. Right, you don't buy it and think it's crappy. Okay, so phase one is a general belief that the product can do what it says it does. Phase two, the conversation looks like this. I believe that that product has the potential to actually do what it says it can. And I believe that I could actually do it too. Okay, this is like deep crap. Awesome. Okay, so we have, we call it the vehicle, the product-based belief. Is a general belief that the product can actually do what it's promising. I believe that that iPhone could actually do what it says it's promising. Then we go to internal beliefs. I believe it can do what it says it's promising and I believe I actually could pull it off also. The third is I believe that product says it can do it, it says it wants to do, that I could do it as well and that I have the resources capable to do it. So we call it the vehicle, the internal and the external. So when you look at what we're doing, yeah exactly, vehicle, internet, so some of you guys are, someone said nope, Android. But for every product and every decision and any hobby you took up, now this is not just in purchase behavior, this is human behavior in any new opportunity that's presented. We have this, I'm not gonna go any deeper than that, but I need you to understand what this is. Vehicle-based belief, meaning the product. So think about a car. I think that that car could actually take me where it says it can. There's the possibility that it could actually deliver. Number two, I believe that I could actually pilot that car. I can get in, I can drive that car. I have the personal capacity to do that. Number three, I believe I have the resources to get where I'm trying to go with that product or in that car. Meaning, do I have the time? Do I have the money? Do I have the support from spouse? Okay, do I have external-based resources, things that are away from me that I know I need on my journey? Okay, vehicle. You guys getting this? So that's the three. Those are the three magic ones and typically the brain experiences, sales and any decision-making in that order, usually. I see there's a possibility that that can happen, that I could pull it off and that I have the resources to do so. That's the conversation.
